intent:
A woman waits with us on the platform; she sets her umbrella down on the path and walks away, leaving it behind; the narrator notices, calls out 「待ってくださいね」 to her on their own initiative, and the woman comes back and takes the umbrella from the narrator's hand. The story stages the corpus's FIRST direct quoted speech, rendered as 「…」 with the verb-of-saying inferred from context — no と言いました framing because と-quotative is N4, above the bootstrap tier.

HISTORICAL NOTE: This story went through three §E.7 fresh-eyes round-trips. The original draft had mother voicing the request (「傘を渡してくださいね」) and the narrator delivering the umbrella to the woman (私は女の人に傘を渡しました). Round 2 of §E.7 flagged TWO problems: (a) the closer mirrored story 4's exact noun-WA-noun-NO-noun-NI-noun-WO-verb-MASHITA template, and (b) the narrator was a passive 'delivery service' executing mother's command, not an agent. Round 2 fix: narrator now voices the call themselves, and the closer flips so the WOMAN is the subject taking the umbrella from the narrator (女の人は私から傘を持ちました — noun-WA-noun-から-noun-WO-verb-MASHITA, structurally distinct from story 4). Round 3 of §E.7 then flagged that the umbrella's appearance on the road was unmotivated — the original had 「女の人は道へ歩きます」 (she walks toward the road) followed by 「傘は道にあって、女の人は持ちません」 (umbrella is on the road, she doesn't hold it), with no causal bridge between the two. Round 3 fix: s3 now explicitly 「女の人は道に傘を置いて、歩きます」 (she sets the umbrella down on the path and walks away), making the drop a deliberate-but-forgetful act with a real verb (置く, N5, the 7th mint) rather than an inferred consequence. Final §E.7 verdict: SHIP. Override budget: 0/1 used.

PREMISE CONTRACT (story 5, FINAL — reconciled with shipped artifact)
  one_sentence_event:    A woman at the station sets her umbrella down on the path and walks away; the narrator calls out for her to wait, and the woman returns and takes the umbrella from the narrator's hand.
  change_of_state:       at s0 the umbrella is offstage; at s2 the woman is holding it in front of me; at s3 she sets it down on the path and starts walking away; at s4 it sits on the path with no one holding it; at the closer it has been re-claimed by the woman, but only because the narrator's call interrupted her departure. Multiple owner-state transitions: held → set-down → unowned-on-ground → reclaimed via the narrator's intervention. The world has materially moved: an object was about to be lost, and a stranger child's voice prevented the loss.
  scene_novelty_claim:   I am NOT writing a {classroom, kitchen, walk_to_shop} story because the last 3 were. Scene is station — first appearance in the v2 corpus, matches the seed-plan slot 5 prescription.
  anchor_novelty_claim:  Anchor is 傘 (umbrella). Last 5 anchors were tea_cup, apple, egg, pencil — umbrella is fresh and the FIRST weather-implement anchor in v2.
  closer_shape_claim:    Closer shape: noun-WA-noun-から-noun-WO-verb-MASHITA (女の人は私から傘を持ちました). Distinct from all three banned shapes AND distinct from story 4's noun-WA-noun-NO-noun-WO-verb-MASHITA (different — story 4 has subject-WA + possessive NO; mine has subject-WA + ablative から). The woman is the SUBJECT of the closer (she takes), not the recipient — the agency flips compared to my round-1 draft, which gives the woman a real moral act in the story rather than reducing her to a prop.
  why_not_filler:        私 — narrator AND voicer of the call (s5) AND the hand the woman takes the umbrella from (s6). The narrator drives the moral act on their own initiative; mother is present but does NOT command. 母 — scene-grounding companion (s0/s1 establish the wait). She is reduced from her role in stories 2/3 (active agent) to a quiet witness here, which is the right register for a child-stranger interaction. 女の人 — the stranger whose presence makes this not a private family scene. She is the umbrella's owner AND the closer's subject AND the moral recipient of the narrator's call; she has full agency in the closer (she returns and takes the umbrella, not the other way around). 傘 — the anchor; it changes location twice (held → set-down → reclaimed) and changes possession-state across 3 sentences, satisfying the object-causality test on multiple counts.
  obligations_absorbed:  G044_te_kudasai (NEW) lands inside s5: 私は「待ってくださいね」 — narrator's own call, not a bolted-on grammar exercise. G034_ne_confirm (NEW) lands inside the same dialogue line, where ね softens the call into a respectful request from a child to an adult stranger. Both new grammar points share one sentence, which is dense but truthful: a child calling out to an unfamiliar adult IS the canonical home of these two particles together (in Japanese, an unsoftened てください from a child to a stranger would read as rude). G036_masen (REINFORCE — must-carry from story 4) lands in s4 (女の人は持ちません — the negation IS the discovery beat, not a bolted-on exercise). G013_mashita_past reinforced via the closer 持ちました. G007_te_form reinforced via s2 (持って), s3 (置いて), s5 (待って — inside the te-kudasai). G008_te_iru reinforced via s2 (持っています). G004_ni_location reinforced via s0/s3/s4. G017_de_means reinforced via s2 (私の前で). G010_to_and reinforced via s0/s1 (companion-と). G006_kara_from reinforced via the closer's 私から (ablative から). All seed-plan grammar lands: te_kudasai (NEW), arimasu_imasu (s0 います, s4 あって — already covered by G021), kara_because is DEFERRED — see deferral note.

  DEFERRAL NOTE: Seed-plan grammar list was {N5_te_kudasai, N5_kara_because, N5_arimasu_imasu}. te_kudasai is introduced. arimasu_imasu maps to G021_aru_iru (already covered by story 1) so it is structurally satisfied via reuse. kara_because (G030_kara_reason — clause-final から after a plain or polite predicate) is DEFERRED to a later story because: (a) the plain-form predicate variant requires G055_plain_nonpast_pair which is locked behind a cascade trap (no story 5–10 currently uses plain dictionary form, so introducing G055 here would create reinforcement debt that no downstream story can satisfy without burning grammar slots — see AGENTS.md G055 cascade rule); and (b) the polite-form variant 「〜ますから」 / 「〜ですから」 fits scenes that have an explicit explanatory beat the narrator must justify, and this station scene's grammar of returning a dropped object is direct enough that an explanatory から would feel bolted on. The kara introduction is best held for a story whose intent NEEDS a stated reason — e.g. 'the bird leaves because the rain comes.'

  In place of the deferred kara_because, I introduce N5_ne_confirm (G034) — the second new grammar point, also from the uncovered N5 list. ね is the canonical sentence-final softener for a child's call to a stranger and lands inside the te_kudasai dialogue with zero scene cost. Total new grammar: 2 (te_kudasai, ne_confirm), which satisfies grammar_min=2.

  CHARACTER NAMING: 'old woman' / おばあさん is the seed-plan archetype but UniDic lacks a clean lemma binding (おばあさん tokenizes as おばあ+さん with the second token marked <TODO> in would-mint output). 老人 is N3 (above bootstrap cap, would burn an override slot). 女の人 is fully N5 (女:N5, 人:N5), tokenizes cleanly, and reads naturally as 'the woman' — slightly less specific than 'old woman' but the umbrella-leaving moment doesn't require the woman's age to land. The seed-plan intent ('old woman drops something') is honored at the level of structure (a stranger, not family; an object lost and returned) without forcing a tokenization workaround. characters_min=3 is satisfied: 私, 母, 女の人.

FORBIDDEN THIS STORY:
  scene_class    (last 3 stories): classroom, kitchen, walk_to_shop
  anchor_object  (last 5 stories): apple, egg, pencil, tea_cup
  closer_shape   (last 3 stories): noun-WA-noun, noun-WA-noun-NO-noun-NI-noun, noun-WA-noun-NO-noun-WO-noun
  opening_seqs   (last 3 stories' first 3 content tokens):
    - 朝 / 母 / 私
    - 朝 / 台所 / 卵
    - 私 / 学校 / います

All four zones honored: scene_class=station (fresh); anchor=傘 (fresh); closer shape is noun-WA-noun-から-noun-WO-verb-MASHITA (woman as subject, ablative から — distinct from all three banned shapes AND from story 4's narrator-as-agent shape); opening sequence is 私 / 母 / 駅 (fresh — and crucially does NOT lead with 朝, breaking the story 2/3 朝-opening cliché AND avoiding the lint 11 lonely-朝 warning that the original draft tripped).

MINT BUDGET: 7 mints exactly (cap is 7). Mints: 駅 (s0), 電車 (s1), 待つ (s1+s5 dedup'd via the 2026-04-29 lemma-keyed dedup fix to one mint), 女 (s2), 人 (s2), 傘 (s2), 置く (s3 — added in §E.7 round 3 to make the drop causally explicit). All 7 are JLPT N5 — no above-cap mints, no overrides consumed.

DIALOGUE STAGING: s5 renders the narrator's call as direct quoted speech inside 「…」 brackets, with the verb-of-saying inferred from context (the narrator is the topic; the bracketed clause is what they say). NO と言いました framing — the と-quotative is N4 (G028_to_iimasu), above the current tier, and would also trip lint 11.4 (companion-と with non-animate clause). The bracket-only convention is how Japanese narrative prose commonly renders speech when the speaker is already established as topic; it stays inside N5.

sentences.en: I am at the station with mother.

sentences.jp: 私は母と電車を待ちます。
sentences.en: I wait for the train with mother.

sentences.jp: 女の人は私の前で傘を持っています。
sentences.en: A woman in front of me is holding an umbrella.

sentences.jp: 女の人は道に傘を置いて、歩きます。
sentences.en: The woman sets the umbrella down on the path and walks away.

sentences.jp: 傘は道にあって、女の人は持ちません。
sentences.en: The umbrella is on the path, and the woman is no longer holding it.

sentences.jp: 私は「待ってくださいね」。
sentences.en: I call out, "Please wait, won't you?"

sentences.jp: 女の人は私から傘を持ちました。
sentences.en: The woman took the umbrella from me.
